article.title: Thoughts on slowing the fuck down
article.thesis: Coding agents are creating brittle, unmaintainable software because developers delegate too much authority without oversight. The solution is maintaining human agency over architecture while using agents only for scoped, evaluable tasks.
key_arguments: Unlike humans, agents don't learn from mistakes—they repeat errors, causing compounding failures at unsustainable rates.
Agents trained on industry practices create unnecessarily complex architectures that humans never designed and can't fully understand.
Software quality is declining: 98% uptime becoming the norm, AWS outages from AI oversight, companies 'agentically coded into a corner.'
examples: AWS outage reportedly caused by AI oversight
Windows quality decline under heavy AI code generation
Companies describing themselves as 'agentically coded into a corner'
article.prescription: Use agents only for scoped, evaluable tasks. Humans must retain control over architecture and critical decisions. Slow down and maintain oversight.

personas.name: The Weary Veteran
personas.description: Experienced devs who've seen hype cycles before and are skeptical of transformation claims

quotes.text: I'm not even sure building software is an engineering discipline at this point. Maybe it never was.
quotes.author: perrygeo

quotes.text: If I engineer a bridge I know the load the bridge is designed to carry.
quotes.author: dstroot

personas.core_argument: Software has never achieved true engineering rigor; AI tools don't change fundamental discipline problems that have existed for decades.

personas.name: The Democratization Believer
personas.description: Sees AI as empowering non-programmers to create software

quotes.text: Artists and non-programmers can now create sophisticated digital tools previously requiring coding knowledge.
quotes.author: HeWhoLurksLate

quotes.text: AI pushes focus from 'building software' to 'solving problems,' benefiting problem-solvers.
quotes.author: crystal_revenge

personas.core_argument: AI democratizes creation—people who understand problems can now build solutions without deep coding expertise.

personas.name: The Skeptical Realist
personas.description: Questions whether AI-built software is actually good or sustainable

quotes.text: Where are the actually *good* applications built by non-experts? Sees only 'parlor tricks' rather than innovative creations.
quotes.author: Forgeties79

quotes.text: Questions sustainability of $20/month AI pricing given industry's ongoing financial losses.
quotes.author: foobarchu

personas.core_argument: Show me the actually good software built this way. Current examples are demos, not production systems. Economics don't support current pricing.

personas.name: The Systems Thinker
personas.description: Focuses on complexity, abstraction layers, and systemic fragility

quotes.text: Complexity creates inevitable failures; questions whether adding AI to fragile systems helps.
quotes.author: PaulHoule

personas.core_argument: Modern software stacks are already dangerously complex. AI adds another layer of opacity to systems we barely understand.

personas.name: The Capitalist Critic
personas.description: Attributes problems to market incentives rather than technology

quotes.text: It's almost as if we lived under capitalism.
quotes.author: lejalv

quotes.text: Every major company's real product is stock price appreciation; actual products are side quests.
quotes.author: ryandrake

personas.core_argument: The real issue isn't AI or methodology—it's that companies optimize for shareholder value, not software quality.

tangents.summary: Extended debate comparing software to civil engineering. Key distinction: bridge engineers face lawsuits; software developers ship post-hoc fixes.
Most basic software categories (communication, delivery, search) already solved. Current work is incremental improvement or recombination.

consensus_points: Software complexity has increased dramatically despite better tooling
The industry generates significant 'meta-work' that doesn't solve user problems
Sustainable pace matters more than specific methodology
open_questions: Will AI pricing remain viable as losses mount?
Can vibe-coded software scale beyond personal/small projects?
Does AI-assisted coding actually change fundamental software quality dynamics?
